sourceMovie.title: Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear (2008) stuck because it commits to its animation premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Yasuichiro Yamamoto's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The animation and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Case Closed: Countdown to Heaven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear clicked for you, Case Closed: Countdown to Heaven (2001) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and action, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear?
faq.answer: Try Case Closed: Countdown to Heaven, Case Closed: The Crimson Love Letter, Case Closed: The Fourteenth Target—each shares DNA with Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear?
faq.answer: Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear (2008) is best described as Animation, Action, Crime, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Detective Conan: Full Score of Fear part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Detective Conan Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
